content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 2, 1999

Steven S. Lucas
Nielsen, Merksamer, Parrinello, Mueller & Naylor, LLP
591 Redwood Highway, #4000
Mill Valley, California 94941

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-059

Dear Mr. Lucas:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Rosa
Councilmember Sharon Wright regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Pursuant to section 87103, does Councilmember Wright have an economic interest in
Keith Christopherson, a volunteer director, president and member of the executive committee of
a nonprofit corporation for which Councilmember Wright provides administrative services as its
executive director?

CONCLUSION

No. Councilmember Wright does not have an economic interest in Mr. Christopherson
for purposes of section 87103. Mr. Christopherson is one of eight people on the executive
committee who controls the contract relationship between Councilmember Wright and SCA.
Under these circumstances, it is not appropriate to pierce through the nonprofit entity to any
single member of the executive committee.

_______________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-99-059
Page 2

FACTS

Councilmember Wright is the owner of Wright & Associates, a California corporation
that contracts to provide administrative services to trade and professional associations. For the
past approximately 12-13 years, Wright & Associates has primarily provided administrative
services to the Sonoma County Alliance ("SCA") under contract. Pursuant to the contract,
Councilmember Wright serves as the executive director of SCA. Wright & Associates receives
contract payments from SCA in excess of $250 per year.

SCA is a voluntary membership organization comprised of a county-wide coalition of
business, agricultural and labor interests who are focused on business and economic issues.
Many businesses located within Sonoma County are members, others are not. SCA is organized
as a California nonprofit corporation under section 501(c)(6) of the Internal Revenue Code.

SCA has a volunteer board of directors of approximately 50 members, a 15-member
volunteer executive committee and a volunteer president. The volunteer president presides over
meetings of the executive committee as well as meetings of the board of directors. The executive
committee, not the volunteer president, makes contracting decisions regarding personnel and
personal services, including the decision to contract with Wright & Associates. Each member of
the executive committee, including the president, is entitled to one vote. Executive committee
action is taken by a majority vote. Proposals for executive committee consideration and action
may be suggested and/or presented by any member of the executive committee and/or by the
executive director.

Each year when the executive committee considers the request for renewal of the SCA
contract for administrative services with Wright & Associates, Councilmember Wright presents
the proposed renewal to the executive committee. The executive committee then considers the
proposal in a closed session with the executive director absent. A new contract is entered into if
a majority of the executive committee approves the contract.

Keith Christopherson is a volunteer member on SCA's board of directors. For the past
few years, Mr. Christopherson has served on SCA's 15-member volunteer executive committee.
On January 1, 1999, Mr. Christopherson became the volunteer president of SCA. On
February 18, 1999, Mr. Christopherson resigned from his position as president, and as a member
of the executive committee. Mr. Christopherson is the sole owner of Christopherson Homes, a
home builder and dues-paying member of SCA.

File No. A-99-059
Page 3

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits public officials from making, participating in making or in any way
attempting to use their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they have
a financial interest. (Section 87100.) Councilmember Wright is a public official for purposes of
the Act. (Section 82048.)

An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official, or on, among other enumerated economic interests: 1) any business
entity in which the official has an investment worth $1,000 or more; 2) any source of income of
$250 or more provided to, received by or promised to the official within 12 months prior to the
time the decision is made; or 3) any business entity in which the official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a), (c) and (d).)

Councilmember Wright is the owner of Wright & Associates, a California corporation
that contracts to provide administrative services to trade and professional associations.
Presumably, she has received $250 or more from the business within the previous 12 months and
her investment interest in the business is worth $1,000 or more. (Section 87103(a) and (c).) As
the owner of the business, the councilmember also has an economic interest in Wright &
Associates under Section 87103(d).

The term "income" includes a pro rata share of any income of a business entity in which
the official owns a 10 percent interest or greater. (Section 82030(a).) Presumably,
Councilmember Wright owns a 10 percent interest or greater in Wright & Associates. As a
source of income of $250 or more to Wright & Associates, SCA is also a source of income to and
therefore an economic interest of the councilmember.

Keith Christopherson is a volunteer member on SCA's board of directors. For the past
few years, Mr. Christopherson has served on SCA's 15-member volunteer executive committee.
On January 11, 1999, Mr. Christopherson became the volunteer president of SCA. When a
nonprofit corporation is a source of income to a public official, the Commission has consistently
advised that neither the members of the nonprofit entity nor its directors are sources of income to
the official simply by virtue of their relationship to the nonprofit corporation. (Bracken Advice
Letter, No. A-98-301; Chouteau Advice Letter, No. A-96-030; Fazio Advice Letter, No. A-93-
442; Herbst Advice Letter, No. I-87-319; Best Advice Letter, No. A-81-032.) Therefore, as a
volunteer director, president and member of the executive committee, Mr. Christopherson is not
a source of income to Councilmember Wright by virtue of his positions.

File No. A-99-059
Page 4

However, we have also advised that where a president/majority shareholder of a closely-
held corporation "controls the employment relationship" between the public official and the
corporation, both the corporation and the majority shareholder are considered sources of income
to the public official. (Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-80-069.) By analogy, we have applied a
similar analysis to controlling members of a nonprofit corporation. (Whittlesey Advice Letter,
No. A-97-552; Allen Advice Letter, No. A-95-188; Lucas Advice Letter, No. A-96-248; Rankin
Advice Letter, No. A-94-310; Galiano Advice Letter, No. I-94-088; Kenny Advice Letter, No.
A-93-470; Abt Advice Letter, No. A-91-361; Nawi Advice Letter, No. 84-013.)

In the Whittlesey letter, a planning commissioner's spouse provided design services to a
nonprofit organization that published a newsletter for its members. One of the directors of the
nonprofit organization was directly involved in a decision before the planning commission. The
director, who was also editor of the organization's newsletter, had participated with one other
person in the decision to hire the planning commissioner's spouse. Under these circumstances,
we pierced through the nonprofit organization and concluded that the director/editor was a source
of income to the planning commissioner.

However, in the Abt letter, we reached a different result. In that letter, an airport
commissioner received income from a nonprofit organization for which he served as president.
His salary was determined by a 30-member board of directors. One of the directors was
indirectly affected by a decision before the airport commissioner. Under these facts, we advised
that the director was not a source of income to the commissioner, since no single director
effectively controlled the employment relationship between the nonprofit organization and the
airport commissioner.

Accordingly, if one or a few members of SCA control the contract relationship between
SCA and Wright & Associates, such members will be considered sources of income to
Councilmember Wright. You indicate that the 15-member executive committee decides whether
to renew the contract with Wright & Associates. Each member is entitled to one vote, but a
majority vote is needed for committee action. Each year, Councilmember Wright presents the
proposed renewal to the executive committee. The executive committee then considers the
proposal in a closed session without her presence. A new contract is entered into if a majority of
the executive committee approves the contract.

Under your facts, at least eight members effectively control SCA's employment
relationship with Wright & Associates. Based on these facts, we do not believe that piercing
through the nonprofit corporation to any single member of the executive committee is
appropriate. Therefore, we find that Councilmember Wright does not have an economic interest
in Mr. Christopherson for purposes of the Act.

File No. A-99-059
Page 5

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
